common_name: Concord Grape (American Blue Grape, Slip-Skin Grape, New England Grape)

planting.note: Plant bare-root vines in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; Concord is the most widely grown home garden grape in North America — a slip-skin American Labrusca grape that produces large clusters of deep blue-purple grapes with the iconic 'Concord grape' flavor universally associated with American grape juice, grape jelly, and Welch's products; best in zones 4–7 where its high chilling requirement is reliably met.

directions: Plant on a trellis or arbor in full sun in well-drained soil; prune heavily each winter — Concord is cane-pruned, leaving 2 strong canes per vine; fruit ripens in September in most zones; Concord is self-fruitful and needs no pollinator; the berries slip easily from their skins ('slip-skin'), which facilitates juice making but means they are not firm enough for shipping. Concord grape is the foundational American table and juice grape, developed in 1849 by Ephraim Wales Bull in Concord, Massachusetts, from a chance seedling of Vitis labrusca — the native American 'fox grape' species of the northeastern forests. Bull spent six years selecting seedlings for fruit quality from a wild Labrusca vine growing on his property before exhibiting the named variety at the Massachusetts Horticultural Society in 1853, where it immediately attracted attention and within decades became the most widely planted grape in the United States. The Concord grape flavor — simultaneously musky, sweet-tart, and intensely 'grapey' in a way that European vinifera grapes are not — became the defining American grape taste through Welch's grape juice (first produced in 1869 to provide an unfermented communion grape drink by Dr. Thomas Welch) and the ubiquitous grape jelly that made the PB&J sandwich a cultural icon. The flavor compound primarily responsible for the characteristic Concord aroma is methyl anthranilate, a naturally occurring ester found at high concentrations in Labrusca-type grapes and essentially absent from European vinifera varieties. Concord is a seeded grape (seeds are a minor inconvenience for fresh eating but irrelevant for juicing and jelly making) producing large, round, blue-black berries in substantial clusters in mid-to-late September. The vines are cold-hardy to -20°F and require 100–150 chill hours, which are met in zones 4–8 but rarely in zones 9+.

common_name: Niagara Grape (White Concord, Golden Grape, American White Table Grape)

planting.note: Plant bare-root vines in early spring; Niagara is the most widely grown white (green-gold) American home garden grape — the white counterpart to Concord with the same Labrusca slip-skin character and musky-sweet flavor but in a golden-green berry; developed in Lockport, New York in 1872 and the standard pairing for Concord in northeastern home vineyards and U-pick operations.

directions: Plant on a sturdy trellis in full sun in well-drained soil; train to a 4-arm Kniffin system or similar; cane-prune in late winter; Niagara ripens 1–2 weeks before Concord in most regions (late August to early September); the berries are pale green to amber-gold at full ripeness, very juicy, with the classic musky Labrusca fragrance and flavor. Niagara is the second most important American Labrusca grape after Concord, developed in Lockport, New York in 1872 by C.L. Hoag and B.W. Clark as a cross of Concord and Cassady — two Labrusca-type grapes. Where Concord produces blue-black fruit with the iconic musky-sweet Concord flavor, Niagara produces pale green to golden-amber fruit with an equivalent but distinct flavor: the same Labrusca 'foxiness' but in a lighter, more delicate package that is closer to traditional white grape flavors when eaten fresh, yet unmistakably American in its musky, aromatic character. Niagara has been widely grown in the northeastern and midwestern states since its introduction and is the standard white-fruited companion to Concord in home vineyards across the grape belt of New York, Pennsylvania, Ohio, Michigan, and similar regions. The grapes are seeded (like Concord) with the same slip-skin characteristic — the skin separates easily from the pulp — and produce excellent fresh-eating fruit, white grape juice (with a distinctive musky-sweet flavor that distinguishes American white juice from European-style products), and white jellies and preserves with a characteristic Labrusca fragrance. The vines are vigorous and highly cold-hardy, equivalent to Concord in frost resistance, and similarly require adequate chilling hours that limit their usefulness in zones 9+.

common_name: Blanc du Bois Grape (Gulf Coast Pierce's-Disease-Resistant White Wine Grape)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown vines in spring in full sun in well-drained soil; Blanc du Bois is the most important white wine grape variety developed specifically for the Gulf Coast and Southeast — bred by Dr. John Mortensen at the University of Florida for resistance to Pierce's disease (the bacterial disease spread by the glassy-winged sharpshooter that kills European vinifera grapes throughout the South) while producing high-quality white wine in the warm, humid Gulf South climate.

directions: Train on a cordon trellis system in full sun; prune in late winter; Blanc du Bois ripens in June–July in the Gulf South, much earlier than northern grapes; the fruit is white-green to golden at maturity and produces crisp, fruity white wine with notes of melon, citrus, and tropical fruit; fruit can also be eaten fresh or made into juice. Blanc du Bois is the most important Gulf Coast white wine grape variety, bred by Dr. John Mortensen at the University of Florida's Levin College of Law agricultural breeding program (USDA-ARS Fort Pierce) specifically for the hot, humid climate of the American Southeast and Gulf South where European vinifera varieties cannot survive. The critical factor in Gulf Coast viticulture is Pierce's disease — a bacterial infection caused by Xylella fastidiosa spread by the glassy-winged sharpshooter leafhopper (Homalodisca vitripennis) — which kills European vinifera varieties (Chardonnay, Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, etc.) within 1–5 years throughout the Gulf South. Blanc du Bois was developed as a complex hybrid incorporating Vitis champinii (heat-tolerant wild Texas grape) and other native American species to provide Pierce's disease resistance, while retaining enough vinifera quality characteristics to produce high-quality white wine comparable to European-style dry whites. The resulting wine is crisp, fruity, and aromatic with Muscat-like floral notes and good acidity — genuinely world-class for a Pierce's disease-resistant variety and the backbone of the emerging Gulf Coast wine industry. Blanc du Bois grapes ripen unusually early — in June to early July in zone 9a, compared to September ripening in northern vineyards — which allows harvest before the peak summer heat and humidity. Several Texas and Florida wineries produce award-winning Blanc du Bois wines that have helped establish the variety's reputation nationally.
sources: Gulf Coast Oenological and Viticultural Association

common_name: Black Spanish Grape (Lenoir Grape, Jacques Grape, Historic Gulf Coast Red Grape)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container vines in spring in full sun in any well-drained soil; Black Spanish (Lenoir) is one of the oldest cultivated grapes in Texas and the Gulf South — a Vitis aestivalis hybrid of ambiguous parentage that has been grown in Louisiana and Texas since at least the 1800s and demonstrates exceptional Pierce's disease resistance combined with heat tolerance, making it the most historically important red wine grape of the Gulf South.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil — Black Spanish is notably drought-tolerant once established and thrives in the thin, rocky soils of the Texas Hill Country; train on cordon system; prune in late winter; fruit ripens in July–August in the Gulf South; the small, dark blue-black berries are very juicy with moderate tannins and produce a robust, full-bodied red wine and excellent red grape juice. Black Spanish (also called Lenoir, after a French viticulturalist named Lenoir who promoted it, or 'Jacques' after a French settler who may have introduced it) is a historic American hybrid grape variety that has been cultivated in Louisiana, Texas, and the broader Gulf South since before the Civil War — one of the earliest wine grapes to be grown commercially in Texas. Its precise parentage is disputed (likely a complex hybrid of Vitis aestivalis or Vitis berlandieri with other American species, possibly with some vinifera ancestry from early French colonial plantings), but its performance characteristics are well-established: Black Spanish is exceptionally tolerant of Pierce's disease, heat, and drought. In the 19th century, when European settlers attempted to establish vineyards in the South with vinifera varieties, virtually all of these plantings failed within a few years due to Pierce's disease — but varieties like Black Spanish, which incorporated native American species with natural resistance, survived and produced fruit year after year. This made Black Spanish the cornerstone of the early Texas and Louisiana wine industries, and it remained the dominant red grape in Texas through much of the early 20th century. The wine produced is dark, deeply colored, with robust tannins and an earthy, slightly rustic character that is quite different from European reds but has its own distinctive appeal. Modern Gulf Coast winemakers continue to work with Black Spanish as a component of blended wines.
sources: Texas Wine and Grape Growers Association

common_name: Reliance Seedless Grape (Red Seedless Home Garden Grape, Hardy Seedless)

planting.note: Plant bare-root vines or container plants in early spring in full sun in well-drained soil; Reliance is the most widely planted seedless red table grape for home gardens in the northern and central United States, bred by James Moore at the University of Arkansas for its combination of seedlessness, cold-hardiness (to -15°F), excellent fresh-eating quality, and reliable production in zones 4–8 — filling the niche for an easy-to-eat, sweet, seedless home grape in climates where the Thompson Seedless (a warm-climate variety) is not reliably productive.

directions: Plant on a sturdy trellis in full sun; spur-prune in late winter to 2 buds per spur; Reliance is self-fruitful; fruit ripens mid-August in most zones; the medium-sized, rose-red to red berries are seedless, tender-skinned, and sweet with a mild, pleasant flavor more subtle than Concord; excellent for fresh eating, fruit salads, and home winemaking. Reliance seedless grape is a University of Arkansas-bred red seedless table grape (released 1982, bred by James Moore) that became the most widely planted cold-hardy seedless home garden grape in the United States, filling a genuine need for a sweet, seedless fresh-eating grape that could be grown reliably in zones 4–8 where the California-bred Thompson Seedless and Flame Red Seedless (developed for California's mild winters and low humidity) perform poorly or inconsistently. The breeding background of Reliance includes multiple American Labrusca and hybrid species crosses selected for cold-hardiness, which gives the variety a very cold-tolerant profile — mature canes are hardy to -15°F and the variety tolerates late spring frosts reasonably well. The berries are medium-sized, red-rose to medium red (lighter than dark blue varieties like Mars), round to slightly oval, and truly seedless with a tender, non-slip skin (unlike the slip-skin character of Concord and Niagara). The flavor is sweet, mild, and pleasantly fruity without the strong Labrusca 'foxiness' of slip-skin varieties — closer to the flavor profile of commercial seedless grapes but with its own mild aromatic quality. Reliance is a productive, reliable variety that earns its place as the standard seedless home garden grape for most of the northern and central United States.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture
